symptoms.symptom_name: Abrupt-onset regular very fast palpitations at rest

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Abrupt onset/offset of a regular fast tachycardia is characteristic of re-entrant SVT.
Sinus tachycardia has a gradual onset and warms up/down; it does not start abruptly at rest.
Reduced diastolic filling at very high rates lowers cardiac output, causing presyncope; a marker of haemodynamic effect.
Non-specific — reflects reduced cardiac reserve at the elevated rate.

symptoms.symptom_name: Recurrent brief self-terminating palpitation episodes over the past year

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A history of recurrent abrupt, self-terminating paroxysms is the hallmark of a re-entrant SVT.

symptoms.symptom_name: Current episode sustained >30 minutes, not self-terminating

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A sustained SVT that will not break requires prompt termination and monitored assessment — the reason this presentation is urgent, not benign.

symptoms.symptom_name: Anxiety and diaphoresis during the episode

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Red-herring: anxiety and sweating accompany SVT itself (catecholamine surge) and do not indicate a primary anxiety disorder.
Caffeine can lower the threshold for arrhythmia and cause sinus tachycardia — the red herring; it does not explain an abrupt regular rhythm at 180.

symptoms.symptom_name: No chest pain and no syncope

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of chest pain and syncope, with preserved consciousness, supports current haemodynamic stability.
Haemodynamic stability while conversing at 180 bpm makes a narrow-complex SVT more likely than VT.

symptoms.symptom_name: No regular medications and no recreational stimulant use

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: No illicit stimulant or prescribed pro-arrhythmic drug to account for the rhythm.
A patient-obtainable wearable rate corroborating ~180 bpm; unverified, cannot show the electrical pattern, but consistent with SVT.

symptoms.symptom_name: Regular pulse at approximately 180 bpm on clinician examination

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A perfectly regular rapid pulse supports a regular SVT; clinician-measured, sealed.
AF is irregularly irregular; a perfectly regular pulse argues against it.

symptoms.symptom_name: Blood pressure 115/75 — normotensive at high rate

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Preserved blood pressure indicates a stable rather than unstable tachyarrhythmia — clinician-measured, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Structurally normal cardiovascular examination — no murmurs, clicks or gallops; clear lungs, no heart failure

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A structurally normal heart in a young patient lowers the probability of VT and favours a benign-substrate SVT.

symptoms.symptom_name: Short PR interval <120 ms with narrow QRS and no delta wave on ECG

investigation_details.expected_result: Regular narrow-complex tachycardia during the episode; resting trace shows a short PR (<120 ms), narrow QRS and no delta wave (LGL pattern)
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Without a 12-lead ECG the rhythm cannot be characterised and the pathway (LGL vs AVNRT vs WPW) cannot be distinguished, and AF/flutter cannot be excluded.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Short PR <120 ms with a normal narrow QRS and no delta wave is the defining ECG signature of LGL (distinct from the delta wave of WPW).
Absence of a delta wave argues against a manifest accessory pathway (WPW).

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Abrupt-onset regular fast palpitations, a history of recurrent self-terminating paroxysms, a sustained current episode, and a perfectly regular rapid pulse together strongly indicate a re-entrant SVT.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The single most important investigation — characterises the rhythm, excludes AF/flutter and VT, and distinguishes LGL from WPW. Must be captured while the heart is still racing.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Continuous cardiac (telemetry) monitoring in a monitored setting
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Allows safe vagal manoeuvre and, if needed, IV adenosine or synchronised cardioversion under supervision.
Screen for reversible contributors (e.g. thyrotoxicosis, electrolyte disturbance) to recurrent tachyarrhythmia.
Definitive characterisation of the pathway and potential curative catheter ablation — an outpatient cardiology/EP step, not an acute telehealth action.
No features of acute coronary syndrome in a young patient with a regular narrow-complex tachycardia; over-ordering an ischaemia work-up is unnecessary here.
